EWU0STsAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Architecture, Memory Systems, Hardware Security, Quantum Computing, Phase-Change Memory (PCM), Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)/NVRAM, Cache Partitioning and Replacement Policies, DRAM Reliability and Rowhammer Mitigation, Secure Memory and Memory Encryption, Quantum Error Correction and NISQ Reliability
EWU0STsAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Moinuddin Qureshi is a **Professor** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where he leads a research group focused on advanced topics in **computer architecture** and **memory systems** (see his homepage: https://moin.cc.gatech.edu/). His work spans design and evaluation of novel memory technologies, practical cache and coherence mechanisms, and hardware-oriented security for modern processors. Across these topics he emphasizes solutions that are both high-performance and implementable in real systems, with particular attention to <u>non-volatile memories</u>, <u>last-level cache management</u>, and <u>reliability and security trade-offs</u> in hardware.  

---

## Research Areas
Qureshi’s research program centers on the architecture and systems-level challenges of modern memory hierarchies and secure hardware. He has led foundational work on emerging **phase-change memory (PCM)** as a main-memory technology—exploring system architectures, write/read asymmetry mitigation, multi-level cell exploitation, and wear-leveling techniques (e.g., "Scalable high performance main memory system using phase-change memory technology" (2009); "Enhancing lifetime and security of PCM-based main memory with start-gap wear leveling" (2009); "Morphable memory system" (2010); "PreSET" (2012)). In cache and on-chip memory management, he developed low-overhead runtime partitioning and adaptive replacement/insertion policies to improve multi-application and CMP behavior ("Utility-based cache partitioning" (2006); "Adaptive insertion policies for high performance caching" (2007); "A case for MLP-aware cache replacement" (2006); "The V-Way cache" (2005)). He has also contributed to stacked-DRAM and DRAM-cache designs including fast intra-DRAM data movement ("Low-cost inter-linked subarrays (LISA)" (2016)) and practical DRAM-cache tradeoffs ("Fundamental latency trade-off in architecting dram caches" (2012)).  

Security and reliability are recurring themes: Qureshi’s group proposes defenses against conflict-based cache side channels (e.g., "CEASER" (2018), "MIRAGE" (2021)), secure-memory integrity structures with low overhead ("Morphable counters" (2018), "Synergy" (2018)), and mitigations for DRAM vulnerabilities such as RowHammer and refresh strategies ("Architectural support for mitigating row hammering in DRAM memories" (2014); "A case for refresh pausing in DRAM memory systems" (2013)). More recently, he has extended his architectural expertise to the quantum computing stack—addressing NISQ-era variability, multi-programming, measurement-error mitigation, and error-decoding for fault-tolerant quantum systems ("Not all qubits are created equal" (2019); "A case for multi-programming quantum computers" (2019); "Afs: Accurate, fast, and scalable error-decoding for fault-tolerant quantum computers" (2022)). His portfolio also touches on broader system topics such as NVRAM-aware transaction logging and SSD virtualization.

---

## Notable Works
- Scalable high-performance main memory with PCM — "Scalable high performance main memory system using phase-change memory technology" (2009): a seminal systems-level study that catalyzed follow-on work on PCM architectures, endurance mitigation, and hybrid memory designs, showing how PCM can be integrated as a practical, high-capacity main memory.  
- Low-overhead runtime cache partitioning — "Utility-based cache partitioning: A low-overhead, high-performance, runtime mechanism to partition shared caches" (2006): introduced a practical utility-driven mechanism for dividing shared last-level caches among applications, influencing cache management in CMPs and multi-tenant systems.  
- Practical defenses for cache side channels — "CEASER: Mitigating conflict-based cache attacks via encrypted-address and remapping" (2018): a widely cited, implementable approach to defend shared caches against conflict-based side-channel attacks by combining address encryption and remapping strategies.

---

## Academic Background
Moinuddin Qureshi is a long-standing contributor to the computer-architecture community, serving as a faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology and directing a research group that spans **memory systems**, **hardware security**, and emerging **quantum computing** architectures. His publication record (total citations ~17,670) demonstrates sustained impact across diverse subfields, from early influential cache and CMP studies (mid-2000s) to later work on non-volatile memories, secure-memory designs, and NISQ-era quantum reliability. He collaborates broadly with academic and industrial partners, advises graduate students and postdoctoral researchers, and disseminates his work through top-tier conferences and journals in architecture, systems, and security.

F0HhkJIAAAAJ.keywords: Electrical and Computer Engineering, Computing Diversity, Context-Aware Computing, Availability Prediction, Family Communication Systems, Ubiquitous Computing, Collaborative Augmented Reality, Human-Computer Interaction, Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning, Computing Education and Retention
F0HhkJIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Kristine Nagel is a researcher and educator at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, whose work spans **human–computer interaction**, **context-aware systems**, and *computing diversity and retention*. Her scholarship brings together technical design and empirical study to address how people communicate and learn in everyday environments. Nagel’s work is characterized by an interest in <u>context-aware communication</u> (how environments and routines influence signaling and availability), <u>collaborative augmented reality</u> (how people create and interpret references in mixed physical/virtual spaces), and interventions to improve persistence and engagement for *underrepresented students in computing*.

---

## Research Areas
Nagel’s research portfolio bridges HCI, ubiquitous computing, and computing education. In the HCI and ubiquitous computing strand she has examined predictors of availability and designs for family- and home-focused communication systems (for example, studies on *availability in home life* and designs for home intercom/availability services). In collaborative systems she has investigated the mechanics of referencing and pointing in shared augmented reality environments, including empirical work on the effectiveness of virtual pointers and the broader design space for referencing. In computing education and diversity she has worked on course and programmatic interventions—service-learning courses, programming boot camps, and national community-building efforts like the STARS Computing Corps—to increase confidence, engagement, and retention for women and other underrepresented student groups. Across these areas her methods include design prototyping, controlled and field studies, and program evaluation.

---

## Notable Works
- "Predictors of availability in home life context-mediated communication" (2004) — an influential empirical study identifying household activities and contexts that predict when family members are likely to be available, informing designs for <u>context-aware availability indicators</u>.
- "The design discussion area: A collaborative learning tool in support of learning from problem-solving and design activities" (1999) — a contribution to computer-supported collaborative learning that describes the DDA scaffolding to help students write and reflect on design efforts, supporting collaborative reflection and learning.
- "Understanding the design space of referencing in collaborative augmented reality environments" (2007) — a systematic exploration of how people generate and interpret references in mixed reality collaboration and a foundation for subsequent work on virtual pointers and AR-mediated collaboration.

---

## Academic Background
Nagel has a long-standing academic and research trajectory spanning interdisciplinary work in electrical/computer engineering, human–computer interaction, and computing education. Her publication record stretches back to the late 1990s and includes collaborations on both technical design studies and educational interventions. She has been affiliated with the Georgia Institute of Technology and has contributed to programmatic efforts at regional institutions (e.g., work tied to Georgia Gwinnett College) and to national broadbanding-participation initiatives such as the STARS Computing Corps. Her work has been cited widely (total citations: **413**), and she has led and participated in externally funded projects and curricular innovations focused on <u>retention and persistence</u> for underrepresented students in computing, service-learning, and outreach (including summer camps and boot camps) while maintaining a strong research program in context-aware communication and collaborative AR.

Lxv3W74AAAAJ.keywords: Scientific Machine Learning, Large-scale Optimization, Numerical Methods, Inverse Design in Optics, Metasurfaces, Topology Optimization, Physics-informed Neural Networks, Neural Operators, PDE Surrogate Modeling, Computational Imaging
Lxv3W74AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Raphael Pestourie is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computational Science and Engineering at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of *computational photonics* and *scientific machine learning*. His work centers on the development of scalable numerical methods and optimization frameworks for the *inverse design* of complex optical systems. Key emphases of his group include the integration of **physics-informed machine learning**, fast and accurate **surrogate models** for partial differential equations, and large-scale **topology optimization** methods to enable the design of ultra-large-area devices such as metasurfaces and meta-optics. Across his publications he advances both foundational algorithms (e.g., neural operators, multifidelity surrogates, active learning) and practical design workflows for optical hardware, with attention to <u>inverse design</u>, <u>metasurfaces</u>, and <u>physics-aware deep surrogates</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Pestourie’s research spans several tightly connected areas: scalable inverse design for nanophotonics and meta-optics, scientific machine learning for PDE-based physics, and large-scale optimization / numerical methods. He develops and applies methods such as **physics‑informed neural networks with hard constraints** (see "Physics-informed neural networks with hard constraints for inverse design", 2021) and **deep neural operators / multifidelity operator learning** ("Multifidelity deep neural operators for efficient learning of partial differential equations", 2022) to accelerate forward and inverse solvers. In the optics domain he has advanced computational frameworks for the inverse design of <u>large-area metasurfaces</u> and freeform meta-optics (e.g., "Inverse design of large-area metasurfaces", 2018; "Topology optimization of freeform large-area metasurfaces", 2019), and developed end-to-end co‑design approaches that jointly optimize nanophotonic front‑ends and computational back‑ends for imaging and polarimetry ("End-to-end nanophotonic inverse design for imaging and polarimetry", 2021; "End-to-end metasurface inverse design for single-shot multi-channel imaging", 2022). He also contributes methodological advances in active learning and physics-enhanced surrogates for efficient design-loop iteration ("Active learning of deep surrogates for PDEs", 2020; "Physics-enhanced deep surrogates for partial differential equations", 2023), enabling practical inverse design at scales of 10^5–10^6 degrees of freedom.

---

## Notable Works
- "Physics-informed neural networks with hard constraints for inverse design" (2021) — Introduced a rigorous approach to embed hard physical constraints into physics‑informed neural networks, improving stability and reliability for inverse-design problems across acoustics, mechanics, transport, and optics; this work established a widely cited bridge between constrained PDE learning and practical design tasks (1024 citations).  
- "Inverse design of large-area metasurfaces" (2018) — Developed a scalable computational framework for optimization-based inverse design of metasurfaces spanning thousands of wavelengths, enabling multi-wavelength and multi-angle optimizations that were previously intractable for ultra-large-area devices (337 citations).  
- "Inverse design enables large-scale high-performance meta-optics reshaping virtual reality" (2022) — Demonstrated how inverse-design pipelines and fast approximate solvers can produce large-scale high-performance meta-optics suitable for demanding applications (e.g., virtual reality), showcasing both algorithmic innovation and experimental relevance (307 citations).

---

## Academic Background
Raphael Pestourie trained at the confluence of computational science, optics, and machine learning and is currently an Assistant Professor at the School of Computational Science and Engineering, Georgia Tech. His publication record—nearly **3,000 citations** to date—reflects sustained contributions to both methodological foundations (scientific machine learning, neural operators, physics‑aware surrogates) and applied nanophotonics (metasurface and meta-optics inverse design). His work has been produced in collaboration with leading research groups and institutions in computational photonics and has informed experimental demonstrations and device prototyping. Pestourie’s portfolio includes multi-institutional collaborations, contributions to patents and translational projects, and a stream of high-impact conference and journal articles that position him as a prominent figure at the interface of *large-scale optimization*, *numerical methods*, and <u>inverse design in optics</u>. For details on his education, specific awards, and current group activities, see his homepage: http://www.raphaelpestourie.com/.

Q6F3O0sAAAAJ.keywords: robotics, computer graphics, machine learning, deep reinforcement learning, legged locomotion, quadrupedal robots, visual navigation, robot design optimization, sim-to-real transfer, safe reinforcement learning
Q6F3O0sAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Sehoon Ha is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working at the intersection of **robotics**, **computer graphics**, and **machine learning**. He is affiliated with and collaborates closely with Georgia Tech research groups in robotics and graphics (including contributors to the *Graphics Lab* and *Humanoid Robotics Lab*), and maintains an active research webpage at http://cc.gatech.edu/~sha9. His primary research focus is on developing data-driven and simulation-informed methods for robust robot control and design, especially for <u>legged locomotion</u>, <u>vision-guided navigation</u>, and the integration of <u>differentiable simulation</u> and <u>reinforcement learning</u> into real-world systems. He combines principled algorithmic development (e.g., advances in policy optimization and meta-learning) with practical tool-building (e.g., open-source toolkits and benchmarks) to move learned robotic behaviors from simulation to hardware with *safety*, *sample efficiency*, and *transferability* in mind.

---

## Research Areas
Sehoon Ha’s work spans foundational and applied topics where control, learning, and design meet. He has contributed to the theory and practice of *model-free deep reinforcement learning* (notably through work on **soft actor-critic** formulations and their applications), and to applying these methods to challenging embodied tasks such as quadrupedal and humanoid locomotion (e.g., "Learning to walk via deep reinforcement learning", "Learning to walk in the real world with minimal human effort"). He studies perception–action integration for locomotion and navigation, developing vision-guided policies for traversing complex terrains ("Visual-locomotion", "ViNL") and semantic/zero-shot navigation methods that combine language and vision ("VLFM"). Ha also works on co-optimization of robot morphology and motion using differentiable and implicit-function-based techniques ("Computational co-optimization...", "Joint Optimization ... using the Implicit Function Theorem"), and on making simulation more useful for learning via differentiable simulators and policy optimization frameworks ("PODS: Policy Optimization via Differentiable Simulation"). Safety, sample-efficiency, and sim-to-real transfer are recurring themes: he has investigated safe RL architectures (e.g., safety critics), meta-learning for fast adaptation, fine-tuning policies on hardware, and metrics/benchmarks that improve the evaluation and deployment of robot navigation systems (e.g., SCT and HM3D-OVON benchmarks).

---

## Notable Works
- "Soft actor-critic algorithms and applications" (2018) — a high-impact contribution to *stochastic actor-critic* methods and practical RL algorithms that trade off exploration and stability; has been widely adopted for continuous-control problems and later applied across his robotics work.  
- "Learning to walk via deep reinforcement learning" (2018) / "Learning to walk in the real world with minimal human effort" (2020) — a suite of papers demonstrating how deep RL can produce robust locomotion controllers and how policies can be adapted and deployed on real legged robots with reduced human intervention.  
- "DART: Dynamic Animation and Robotics Toolkit" (2018) — an open-source simulation and dynamics toolkit developed with Georgia Tech collaborators that supports research in animation, control, and robot design; this engineering contribution underpins many simulation-to-real workflows used in his subsequent papers.

---

## Academic Background
Sehoon Ha is a widely cited researcher (total citations ≈ 9,264) whose publications bridge machine learning, robotics, and graphics communities. His publication record shows long-term collaborations with Georgia Tech research labs and external groups (e.g., University of Washington contributors to DART), and includes both algorithmic advances and community resources (toolkits and benchmarks). His work has been published in top venues across robotics, machine learning, and computer graphics and has influenced directions in legged locomotion, safe and sample-efficient RL, differentiable simulation, and embodied vision-and-language navigation. He has led and co-authored multiple multidisciplinary projects that combine software tool development, large-scale benchmark construction, and hardware validation, and he routinely mentors students and collaborators in translating learned policies from simulation into robust real-world robotic systems.

Q_4d9N0AAAAJ.keywords: Computer Graphics, Robotics, Scientific Visualization, Biological Simulation, Surface Reconstruction, Mesh Processing and Simplification, Texture Synthesis, Implicit Surface Modeling, Fluid and Deformable Simulation, Sim-to-Real Robot Learning
Q_4d9N0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Greg Turk is a **Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group working at the intersection of **computer graphics**, **scientific visualization**, and *robotics-enabled simulation*. His work spans foundational algorithms for geometric processing and surface modeling, to physically based animation and data-driven approaches for control and locomotion. Across these efforts he focuses on <u>robust surface reconstruction and representation</u>, <u>example-based texture and pattern synthesis</u>, and <u>simulation-to-real transfer for robot learning</u>, producing techniques that are both theoretically grounded and practical for applications in graphics, visualization, and robotics.

---

## Research Areas
Turk's research covers several tightly connected areas of computer graphics and simulation. In geometric processing and surface reconstruction he developed methods for combining range scans into coherent polygonal models (e.g., "Zippered polygon meshes from range images", 1994) and for building multi-resolution implicit representations ("Multi-level partition of unity implicits", 2005; "Variational implicit surfaces", 1999). In mesh processing and level-of-detail he produced influential work on re-tiling and simplification ("Re-tiling polygonal surfaces", 1992; "Simplification envelopes", 1996; "Fast and memory efficient polygonal simplification", 1998). Turk has made major contributions to texture synthesis and surface-driven appearance — from biologically inspired reaction-diffusion textures ("Generating textures on arbitrary surfaces using reaction-diffusion", 1991) to patch-based image and video synthesis using graph cuts ("Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts", 2003) and texture synthesis directly on surfaces ("Texture synthesis on surfaces", 2001). On the simulation and animation side he has advanced fluid–solid interaction ("Rigid fluid", 2004), particle-based surface reconstruction ("Reconstructing surfaces of particle-based fluids", 2013), and material deformation and fracture ("Animation of fracture by physical modeling", 1991; "Fast viscoelastic behavior with thin features", 2008). More recently his work has expanded into robotics and learning for control, producing methods for universal policies, randomized-simulation training, and sim-to-real transfer for locomotion ("Preparing for the unknown: Learning a universal policy with online system identification", 2017; "Robot learning from randomized simulations: A review", 2022; "Sim-to-real transfer for biped locomotion", 2019).

---

## Notable Works
- "Zippered polygon meshes from range images" (1994) — introduced a practical and robust pipeline for merging multiple range scans into a single, watertight polygonal mesh, enabling high-quality 3D digitization and serving as a foundation for many subsequent reconstruction systems.
- "Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts" (2003) — presented a patch-based texture synthesis method that uses graph cut optimization to find optimal stitching seams, dramatically improving perceptual continuity in synthesized images and videos and influencing a generation of example-based synthesis algorithms.
- "Multi-level partition of unity implicits" (2005) — proposed a scalable implicit surface representation built from local approximants combined by a partition of unity, enabling reconstruction and modeling from very large point sets while supporting multi-resolution editing and robust handling of noise.

---

## Academic Background
Greg Turk has been an active researcher since the late 1980s and is a long-standing member of the Georgia Tech faculty in the College of Computing. Over a multi-decade career he has published widely on graphics, visualization, simulation, and robotics, accumulating over 24,500 citations and shaping both theory and practice in these fields. His publication record shows sustained participation in core conferences and journals of computer graphics and visualization, and his work has been adopted in areas ranging from 3D scanning and mesh processing to texture synthesis and robot learning. Turk's interdisciplinary contributions link geometric modeling, physical simulation, and data-driven learning, and he has collaborated extensively across graphics, vision, and robotics communities while mentoring students who continue to influence these research areas.

Q_Pud88AAAAJ.keywords: active learning, experiment design, data-centric machine learning, concept bottleneck models, multimodal datasets, data subset selection, label-efficient learning, weak supervision, concept-based interpretability, maximum inner product search
Q_Pud88AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Stephen Mussmann is an **Assistant Professor** in Computer Science at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on problems at the intersection of *label-efficient learning*, *dataset design*, and *scalable data selection*. His lab develops methods and tools that enable practitioners to build high-performance models with fewer labels and more principled datasets. Across theory, systems, and empirical benchmarks, Mussmann’s work emphasizes <u>active learning</u>, <u>experimental design</u>, and <u>data-centric machine learning</u>—producing both foundational analyses (e.g., on uncertainty sampling and expected error reduction) and practical artifacts (e.g., LabelBench, VOCALExplore, and DataComp). His research output has garnered substantial community impact (total citations ≈ **3,483**) and spans influential publications on interpretability, dataset construction, and efficient training.

---

## Research Areas
Mussmann’s research sits at the nexus of adaptive data collection, dataset engineering, and efficient model training. He studies theoretical and empirical properties of adaptive sampling and selection methods—examining when and why approaches such as *uncertainty sampling* and *expected error reduction* yield label savings (see papers like “On the relationship between data efficiency and error for uncertainty sampling” and “Active learning with expected error reduction”). Complementing theory, he designs scalable selection algorithms that are practical for deep learning (“Selection via proxy: Efficient data selection for deep learning” / “Select Via Proxy”), and he builds benchmarking and tooling infrastructure for label-efficient workflows (e.g., “Labelbench: A comprehensive framework for benchmarking adaptive label-efficient learning” and “VOCALExplore: Pay-as-you-go video data exploration and model building”). Mussmann also advances the emerging area of dataset-centric research—proposing testbeds and curricula for designing multimodal datasets in “Datacomp: In search of the next generation of multimodal datasets”—and contributes to methods for interpretable, concept-level interaction with models (“Concept bottleneck models”). More recently, he has worked on principled experimental design for modern large language model finetuning and continuous nonlinear optimal experimental design, connecting classical design-of-experiments ideas to contemporary machine learning practice.

---

## Notable Works
- “Concept bottleneck models” (2020) — Introduced and analyzed models that expose and permit manipulation of high-level concepts to improve interpretability and controllability of predictions; this work has strongly influenced research on concept-based explanations and human–model interaction.  
- “Datacomp: In search of the next generation of multimodal datasets” (2023) — Proposed a systematic testbed and evaluation protocol for multimodal dataset design, advocating for dataset-centered rigor comparable to model and optimizer research.  
- “Selection via proxy: Efficient data selection for deep learning” (2019) — Developed practical algorithms for instance selection that reduce labeling and computation costs for large-scale deep learning by leveraging proxy features and cheaper representations.

---

## Academic Background
Stephen Mussmann’s publication record demonstrates a blend of theoretical foundations, systems-building, and empirical benchmarking developed over more than a decade of research activity. He is currently an Assistant Professor in the School of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where he directs a research group working on label-efficient machine learning, dataset design, and experimental design for modern models. His body of work—spanning top venues and multiple influential tool releases—has attracted widespread citations (≈ **3.5k**) and shaped directions in both active learning theory and data-centric ML practice. Mussmann’s projects have produced widely used code and benchmarks (e.g., LabelBench, VOCALExplore, DataComp), reflecting a commitment to reproducibility and practitioner impact. For full details on his education, prior positions, and current team, see his homepage: https://steve.mussmann.us/.

Y67rbX0AAAAJ.keywords: Human-Computer Interaction, Culturally Relevent CS Education, AI education, K-12 AI curriculum, Middle school CS education, Teacher professional development, Assessment of AI learning, Place-based education, Hawaiian immersion (Kaiapuni), Batteryless embedded systems
Y67rbX0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
William Gelder is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** whose work sits at the intersection of **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)** and *computer science education*. He collaborates with K–12 educators, school districts, and multidisciplinary research teams to co-design curricula, assessment tools, and educational technologies. His projects emphasize <u>place‑based and culturally relevant pedagogies</u>, the design and evaluation of age‑appropriate **AI education** for middle school students, and sustainable approaches to ubiquitous sensing and embedded systems. Gelder’s portfolio spans empirical classroom studies, teacher professional development, and systems work that supports new forms of low‑power computing.

---

## Research Areas
Gelder’s research investigates how emerging technologies can be taught, assessed, and responsibly integrated into diverse educational contexts. He studies middle‑school AI curricula and teacher professional development (e.g., co‑designing an AI elective and a PD program for Georgia teachers), methods for measuring student AI knowledge and ethics (design considerations for evaluating middle‑school AI learning), and the design of generative and place‑based educational technology for language immersion schools (work with Hawaiian Kaiapuni educators). Parallel to education‑focused work, he explores sustainable ubiquitous computing through software and systems research, such as *user‑directed assembly code transformations* that enable efficient, batteryless Arduino applications. Across these topics he emphasizes <u>assets‑based</u> and <u>place‑based</u> approaches that center local culture and educator expertise while addressing technical constraints and ethical implications.

---

## Notable Works
- "From lecture hall to homeroom: co-designing an AI elective with middle school CS teachers" (2025) — co‑design study that produced a 9‑week middle school AI elective and accompanying teacher PD, demonstrating models for translating research‑level AI concepts into *age‑appropriate*, classroom‑ready materials.
- "User‑directed Assembly Code Transformations Enabling Efficient Batteryless Arduino Applications" (2024) — systems contribution showing how targeted assembly‑level transformations can reduce energy use and enable viable battery‑free embedded sensing platforms for sustainable ubiquitous computing.
- ""Those don't work for us": An Assets‑Based Approach to Incorporating Emerging Technologies in Viable Hawaiian Teacher Support Tools for Culturally Relevant CS Education" (2024) — ethnographic and design research with Hawaiian immersion schools that establishes principles for creating teacher tools and technologies grounded in local language, culture, and place‑based pedagogy.

---

## Academic Background
Gelder is affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and conducts collaborative research with university partners, state education agencies (including the Georgia Department of Education), and multiple school districts. His publication record from 2024–2025 indicates an early‑career scholar focused on HCI and CS education research, combining classroom fieldwork, co‑design methods, and technical systems work. His projects show sustained partnerships with K–12 teachers (including Hawaiian Kaiapuni immersion schools) and interdisciplinary teams, and his work has accrued a total of **14 citations** to date. Specific degree information and lab affiliation were not provided; his outputs suggest graduate training in areas bridging *computer science, HCI, and education*.

Z6EHuGcAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Networks, Mobile and Wireless Networks, Internet Topology, Humanitarian Computing, Software-Defined Networking, Delay-Tolerant Networks, Message Ferrying, Active Networks, Edge Computing, Peer-to-Peer Networks
Z6EHuGcAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ellen Zegura is a **Professor of Computer Science** at *Georgia Tech*, where she leads a research group in the College of Computing focused on networked systems. Her work sits at the intersection of **computer networks**, <u>Internet topology</u>, and <u>mobile and wireless systems</u>, with a long-standing emphasis on designing models and mechanisms that enable robust connectivity in challenging environments. She has advanced both foundational theory (network modeling and spectral/topological analysis) and applied systems (delay- and disruption-tolerant networking, mobile *message ferrying*, edge computation and *humanitarian computing*), combining measurement, modeling, and protocol design to address problems of connectivity, performance, and resilience.

---

## Research Areas
Zegura’s research spans a set of tightly related areas in networking. She has made foundational contributions to Internet and internetwork modeling—exploring graph-based representations and spectral properties of autonomous-system topologies (e.g., "How to model an internetwork" (1996), "Modeling internet topology" (1997), "Spectral analysis of Internet topologies" (2003)). In mobile and wireless networking she developed and analyzed paradigms for communication in sparse, partitioned environments, such as the *message ferrying* approach and route design in delay-tolerant networks ("A message ferrying approach for data delivery in sparse mobile ad hoc networks" (2004); "Message ferry route design for sparse ad hoc networks with mobile nodes" (2006); multiple DTN power- and energy-management works). Zegura has also influenced programmable and active networking (intellectual histories and architectures, e.g., "The road to SDN" (2014); "Directions in active networks" (2002); "An architecture for active networking" (1997)), and more recently has worked on computation offload and edge-assisted services for mobile devices ("Cosmos" (2014), "Femto clouds" (2015)). Across these topics she blends measurement, design of scalable algorithms and protocols (load balancing, anycasting, caching), and attention to real-world constraints such as energy, intermittency, and humanitarian deployment contexts.

## Notable Works
- "How to model an internetwork" (1996) — a highly cited foundational treatment of graph-based models for internetworks that shaped subsequent empirical and theoretical work on Internet topology and informed topology-aware protocol design.  
- "A message ferrying approach for data delivery in sparse mobile ad hoc networks" (2004) — introduced the message-ferrying paradigm and route/control strategies for delivering data in partitioned/mobile environments, launching a strong line of work on DTNs, ferry route design, and energy-aware mobility control.  
- "The road to SDN: an intellectual history of programmable networks" (2014) — a comprehensive survey and historical analysis that contextualized Software Defined Networking (SDN) within decades of research on programmability and active networks, influencing how researchers and practitioners frame modern programmable-networking efforts.

## Academic Background
Ellen Zegura is a senior faculty member in the School of Computer Science at Georgia Tech with an active research group that has produced influential work since the early 1990s. Her publication record—spanning core networking theory, measurement studies, protocol design, and applied systems for intermittently connected and mobile environments—has accrued well over twenty thousand citations, reflecting sustained impact across the networking community. She has collaborated broadly across academia and industry, mentored multiple generations of graduate students, and contributed service through program committees and editorial roles. Her research has been supported by competitive grants and has bridged traditional networking topics with *humanitarian computing* and edge/mobile computation, underscoring a career that combines rigorous technical contributions with attention to practical deployment challenges.
